---
title: "LeetCode 414. Third Maximum Number"
subtitle: "The Third Largest Number"
lang: en
date: 2026-07-16
author: Anthony
categories: [LeetCode, Array]
description: "[Problem link](https://leetcode.com/problems/third-maximum-number/description/)"
alternate-lang: "/dsa/posts/leetcode/0414-third-max-number/0414-third-max-number.html"
---

Given an integer array, the task is to return the third distinct maximum number in that array. If no third maximum exists, the maximum number should be returned instead, and the solution must run in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time. The constraints are:

- $1 \le \texttt{nums.length} \le 10^4$
- $-2^{31} \le \texttt{nums[i]} \le 2^{31} - 1$

For example, given

```
[3,2,1]
```

the answer is `1`, since `1` is the third largest distinct value in the array. Similarly, for

```
[2,2,3,1]
```

the answer is also `1`. However, given

```
[1,2]
```

the array only has two elements, so a third maximum cannot exist, and the maximum value should be returned instead — in this case, `2`.

## Approach

At first glance, this problem seems to call for a running counter that tracks each number's rank as we traverse the array. That approach, however, runs into a few issues:

1. An element's rank can shift as later values are processed, so its final rank cannot be determined at the moment it is visited.
2. The problem asks for the third **distinct** maximum, so a repeated value must not be counted twice.
3. Comparing every element against every other element naturally leads to a nested loop, which degrades the time complexity to $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$.
4. If fewer than three distinct values exist, the maximum must be returned instead.

Reframing the problem helps: since only the **top three** values matter, it is enough to maintain a candidate array holding at most three elements. For each new number, duplicates are skipped, the value is inserted, and the array is kept in descending order; once the candidate list grows past three, the current smallest value is dropped. Once the traversal is complete, the number of elements remaining in the candidate array determines whether the third maximum or the maximum itself is returned.

## Implementation

The logic is identical across all three languages: skip duplicates while traversing, insert each new number into the candidate array in descending order, discard the smallest value once there are more than three candidates, and finally return either the third maximum or the maximum based on how many candidates remain.

::: {.panel-tabset}
## C

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int thirdMax(int *nums, int numsSize)
{
    int maxNumbers[4]; // [1st max, 2nd max, 3rd max, buffer]
    int count = 0;

    // Traverse all elements
    for (int i = 0; i < numsSize; i++)
    {
        // Check if current num exists in maxNumbers
        // If it exists then skip
        int dup = 0;
        for (int j = 0; j < count; j++)
        {
            if (nums[i] == maxNumbers[j])
            {
                dup = 1;
                break;
            }
        }
        if (dup)
            continue;

        // Push to the end
        maxNumbers[count++] = nums[i];

        // Insertion sort descending: bubble the new element into place
        for (int j = count - 1; j > 0 && maxNumbers[j] > maxNumbers[j - 1]; j--)
        {
            int tmp = maxNumbers[j];
            maxNumbers[j] = maxNumbers[j - 1];
            maxNumbers[j - 1] = tmp;
        }

        // Prune to be 3
        if (count > 3)
            count--;
    }

    return count == 3 ? maxNumbers[2] : maxNumbers[0];
}
```

## C++

```cpp
int thirdMax(vector<int> &nums)
{
    vector<int> maxNumbers; // [1st max, 2nd max, 3rd max]

    // Traverse all elements
    for (size_t i = 0; i < nums.size(); i++)
    {
        // Check if current num exists in maxNumbers
        // If it exits then skip
        if (find(maxNumbers.begin(), maxNumbers.end(), nums[i]) != maxNumbers.end())
            continue;

        // Push and sort descending
        maxNumbers.push_back(nums[i]);
        sort(maxNumbers.rbegin(), maxNumbers.rend());

        // Prune to be 3
        if (maxNumbers.size() > 3)
        {
            maxNumbers.pop_back();
        }
    }
    return maxNumbers.size() == 3 ? maxNumbers[2] : maxNumbers[0];
}
```

## Python

```python
def thirdMax(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
    maxNumbers = []  # [1st max, 2nd max, 3rd max]

    # Traverse all elements
    for i in range(len(nums)):
        # Check if current num exists in maxNumbers
        # If it exists then skip
        if nums[i] in maxNumbers:
            continue

        # Push and sort descending
        maxNumbers.append(nums[i])
        maxNumbers.sort(reverse=True)

        # Prune to be 3
        if len(maxNumbers) > 3:
            maxNumbers.pop()

    return maxNumbers[2] if len(maxNumbers) == 3 else maxNumbers[0]
```
:::
